Gas vs Electric Vehicles: Which Is the Better Choice in 2026?
Feb 3, 2026

As the automotive industry continues to evolve, one of the biggest questions drivers face is whether to choose a traditional gas-powered vehicle or a fully electric vehicle (EV). Both options offer unique advantages, and with advancements in technology, the decision is becoming more relevant than ever for modern drivers.
In 2026, automakers like Chevrolet are producing vehicles that cater to both preferences. From powerful gasoline trucks like the Chevrolet Silverado 1500 to innovative electric models like the Chevrolet Silverado EV, drivers now have more choices than ever before. Understanding the differences between gas and electric vehicles can help buyers decide which option best fits their lifestyle.
Performance and Driving Experience
When it comes to performance, both gas and electric vehicles have strengths depending on what drivers value most.
Gas-powered vehicles have traditionally dominated the market because of their proven reliability and familiar driving experience. Gas engines provide consistent power, long driving ranges, and quick refueling times. Trucks and SUVs powered by gasoline engines remain ideal for drivers who need towing capability or long-distance driving without planning charging stops.
Electric vehicles, however, are known for delivering instant torque and smooth acceleration. Because electric motors produce power immediately, EVs often feel faster off the line than many traditional vehicles. This instant response creates a quiet, smooth, and highly responsive driving experience.
For everyday commuting, electric vehicles often feel more refined, while gas vehicles still excel in heavy-duty tasks and long-distance travel.
Fuel Costs and Efficiency
One of the most significant advantages of electric vehicles is lower operating costs. Charging an EV typically costs less than filling a gasoline tank, especially when charging at home. Over time, this can lead to noticeable savings for drivers who travel frequently.
Electric vehicles are also more energy-efficient than gas vehicles. Electric motors convert a larger percentage of energy into motion, while gas engines lose a significant amount of energy as heat.
Gas vehicles, however, still have an advantage in areas where electricity costs are high or charging infrastructure is limited. Additionally, drivers can refuel a gas vehicle in just a few minutes, while EV charging times can vary depending on the charger type.
For drivers focused on reducing fuel expenses, electric vehicles often provide long-term savings.
Maintenance and Reliability
Another area where electric vehicles stand out is maintenance. EVs have fewer moving parts compared to gas-powered vehicles. They do not require oil changes, spark plugs, or many of the traditional engine maintenance services associated with gasoline engines.
This simpler design often leads to lower long-term maintenance costs.
Gas vehicles, on the other hand, benefit from decades of refinement and a widespread service network. Many drivers are already familiar with maintaining gasoline engines, and repair shops are widely available across the country.
For drivers who want fewer maintenance concerns, electric vehicles can offer a clear advantage.
Environmental Impact
Environmental considerations have become an increasingly important factor in vehicle purchasing decisions. Electric vehicles produce zero tailpipe emissions, which helps reduce air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions.
This makes EVs particularly appealing for drivers who want to reduce their environmental impact.
Gas-powered vehicles still produce emissions during operation, although modern engines have become far more efficient and cleaner than those produced decades ago. Automakers continue improving fuel efficiency and emissions systems to reduce environmental impact.
While electric vehicles are often viewed as the greener option, the overall environmental benefit can depend on how the electricity used for charging is produced.
Charging vs Refueling
One of the biggest differences between gas and electric vehicles is how they are powered.
Gas vehicles rely on fuel stations, which are widely available and allow drivers to refuel quickly. A typical fill-up takes only a few minutes, making gas vehicles extremely convenient for long trips.
Electric vehicles require charging instead of refueling. Many EV owners charge their vehicles overnight at home, similar to charging a smartphone. Public charging stations are also expanding rapidly across the country, making long-distance EV travel more practical than ever before.
However, charging times can still be longer than traditional refueling, depending on the charging speed and battery size.
Which Option Is Right for You?
The choice between gas and electric vehicles ultimately depends on how you plan to use your vehicle.
Drivers who prioritize lower fuel costs, quiet driving, and reduced maintenance may find electric vehicles to be the ideal solution. EVs are especially well suited for daily commuting and city driving.
On the other hand, drivers who need maximum towing capability, long-distance convenience, or quick refueling may prefer a traditional gas-powered vehicle.
Fortunately, modern automakers offer both options. Chevrolet, for example, provides powerful gas trucks alongside innovative electric models, allowing drivers to choose the technology that best fits their needs.
The Future of Driving
The automotive world is entering an exciting period of innovation. Gasoline vehicles continue to evolve with improved efficiency and performance, while electric vehicles introduce new levels of technology and sustainability.
Whether drivers choose gas or electric, today’s vehicles deliver more capability, comfort, and advanced features than ever before. Understanding the benefits of each option can help buyers make a confident and informed decision as the industry continues to move toward the future of transportation.

EV vs Gas Car in 2026: Which Is Better for You?
EV vs Gas Car in 2026: Which Is Better for You?
Table of Contents
If you’re shopping for a car in 2026, you’re almost guaranteed to ask the big question: EV vs gas car, which is better? You’ve heard that electric vehicles are cheaper to run and nicer to drive, but gas cars are familiar, easy to refuel and often cheaper to buy. This guide walks you through the trade-offs using up-to-date 2025–2026 data so you can pick the car that actually fits your life and budget, not the headlines.
Short answer
EV vs gas in 2026 at a glance
Key 2025–2026 numbers that matter
Those numbers set the stage: EVs shine on operating costs, but initial price and resale are more complicated, especially with incentives changing and a wave of off-lease EVs hitting the used market in 2026.
How we’re comparing EVs and gas cars in 2026
What we’ll compare
Assumptions we’ll use
Think total cost of ownership, not just sticker
Purchase price, incentives and financing
Walk into a dealer in 2026 and, model for model, you’ll still usually see a higher sticker price on a new EV than on its gas twin. AAA’s 2025 cost study, for example, pegs the average new EV at around $55,000 vs roughly $48,000 for a gas car, with popular plug‑in hybrids like a RAV4 Prime carrying a five-figure premium over the gas version. That up-front gap is one reason some buyers feel EVs are “too expensive,” even though running costs are lower.
How EV vs gas purchase costs stack up in 2026
Higher sticker price for many EVs, but more ways to bring it down
Sticker price
Gas cars: Usually cheaper to buy new, especially in entry-level trims and basic compact SUVs.
EVs: Still carry a premium in many segments, though price cuts and new models are narrowing the gap.
Incentives & tax credits
Gas cars: No federal purchase incentives.
EVs: Federal and state incentives changed in 2025–2026 and are more limited than a few years ago, but some models and buyers still qualify, and local utilities often offer home charging rebates.
Financing & payments
Both: Lower interest rates and easing new-car prices have brought average monthly payments down a bit from 2024.
EVs: Slightly higher sale price can mean a higher payment, but cheaper running costs can offset that over time.
Watch out for option creep
Fuel vs electricity: what it really costs to drive
Energy is where EVs earn their keep. Over five years at 15,000 miles a year, a typical gas car can burn around $9,000 in fuel at recent U.S. prices, while a comparable EV might use roughly $2,250 in electricity at average residential rates. Off‑peak time‑of‑use plans can knock that closer to $250 per year if you mostly charge overnight at home. Even with swings in gasoline and slight upticks at public fast chargers, electricity remains dramatically cheaper per mile for nearly every U.S. driver.
Sample 5‑year energy costs: EV vs gas (2026 snapshot)
Approximate costs for a typical compact SUV driven 15,000 miles per year, assuming mostly home charging for EV drivers.
| Vehicle type | Energy use | Assumed price | 5‑year energy cost (75,000 miles) | Cost per mile |
 ---  --- 
| Gas compact SUV | 30 mpg, 2,500 gallons | $3.00/gal average | ≈$7,500 | $0.10/mi |
| Efficient gas hybrid | 40 mpg, 1,875 gallons | $3.00/gal average | ≈$5,625 | $0.075/mi |
| EV compact SUV, home charging | 0.30 kWh/mi, 22,500 kWh | $0.15/kWh home average | ≈$3,375 | $0.045/mi |
| EV compact SUV, off‑peak plan | 0.30 kWh/mi, 22,500 kWh | $0.10/kWh off‑peak | ≈$2,250 | $0.03/mi |
Actual costs will vary by state, utility rates, gas prices and how often you use fast charging.
What about public fast charging?
Maintenance, repairs and reliability
Pop the hood on an EV and there’s simply less there to break. No engine, no multi‑speed automatic transmission, no exhaust or emissions hardware. That simplicity shows up in the shop bills: recent analyses suggest EVs require roughly 50% fewer maintenance items over five years compared with gas cars, largely because you never pay for oil changes, spark plugs, timing belts or exhaust repairs. Brakes last longer too, thanks to regenerative braking doing much of the stopping.
Typical maintenance differences: EV vs gas
The hidden cost advantage that shows up year after year
Gas car maintenance
Over 5 years, that often adds up to several thousand dollars, even on a reliable car.
EV maintenance
Owners commonly see maintenance and repair costs in the $1,000–$2,000 range over 5 years, vs well over $4,000 for many gas equivalents.
The elephant in the room: battery replacement
Range, charging and road trips
If your daily routine is a 20‑mile commute, range anxiety is more of a marketing term than a real problem. Most 2025–2026 EVs offer 250–320 miles of EPA‑rated range, and you’ll leave home every morning with a “full tank” if you can charge overnight. The stress shows up when you don’t control where and when you can plug in, apartment parking, street parking, or frequent long highway runs through areas with spotty fast-charger coverage.
Side-by-side icons and charts comparing typical EV and gas car costs, maintenance and energy use for 2026 buyers
Questions to answer before choosing an EV over gas
1. Can you charge where you sleep?
A dedicated outlet or Level 2 charger in a garage or driveway is the single biggest quality-of-life boost for EV ownership. Without it, you’ll lean more on public networks and lose some cost advantage.
2. How often do you road trip?
If you regularly drive 400–600 miles in a day, especially off the interstate grid, a gas car is still simpler. If most drives are under 200 miles, an EV will feel effortless.
3. What’s charging like in your area?
Urban and suburban corridors are steadily adding fast chargers, but some rural regions still have big gaps. Check apps like PlugShare, ChargePoint or Tesla’s map before you commit.
4. Are you okay planning fuel stops?
EV road trips work best when you plan around 20–40 minute charging stops. If you prefer to wing it and stop only when the low-fuel light screams, a gas tank fits that habit better.
Hybrid as a middle ground
Resale value and the 2026 used-EV flood
Depreciation is the single biggest cost of owning any new vehicle, EV or gas. In 2025 and into 2026, we’ve watched something unusual: a wave of off-lease EVs hitting the market just as incentives shrank and some shoppers pulled back. That combination has pushed many used EV prices down faster than equivalent gas models, great news if you’re buying used, not so great if you paid new‑car money two or three years ago.
New EV buyers
Used EV shoppers
How Recharged helps on resale risk
Ready to find your next EV?
Environmental impact, noise and driving feel
Under the skin, EVs and gas cars feel like wildly different tools. An EV delivers instant torque, near‑silent acceleration and one‑pedal driving in traffic. You’ll never smell fuel on your hands at a pump, and your neighbors will appreciate the lack of cold‑start racket at 6 a.m. Tailpipe emissions are zero, and even after accounting for power‑plant generation, most U.S. grids make an EV cleaner over its lifetime than a comparable gas car, especially in regions with lots of renewables.
Who should choose an EV in 2026?
Drivers who usually come out ahead with an EV
If several of these sound like you, the math likely favors electric
You can charge at home
Your driving is predictable
You keep cars 5+ years
You care about emissions
You like tech
You qualify for incentives
Who should stick with gas, for now
Situations where gas still wins in 2026
Why a gas car might be a “bridge” decision
If you’re close to EV‑ready but not quite there, maybe your next apartment will have charging, or your employer is planning to add workplace chargers, a relatively efficient gas or hybrid vehicle can be a smart bridge. You’ll save fuel now while keeping your options open for a used EV later, when infrastructure and prices are even better.
Don’t overbuy range you don’t need
How used EVs change the equation
So far we’ve talked mostly about new vehicles, but 2026 is shaping up to be a breakout year for used EVs. Lease returns from the big 2021–2023 sales push are pouring into the market just as shoppers grow more comfortable with electric and prices soften. In plain English: a lot of very good EVs are suddenly affordable.
Why a used EV can beat a new gas car in 2026
Lower purchase price, same electric benefits
You can often buy a 3‑year‑old EV with modern safety tech and plenty of range for the price of a new entry‑level gas compact, and still enjoy cheap electricity and low maintenance.
Depreciation has already done its worst
The steepest value drop usually happens in the first few years. By buying used, you let the first owner take that hit and you enjoy a slower glide afterwards.
Battery health can be measured
You no longer have to guess. Tools like the Recharged Score battery health diagnostics use real data to show you how much usable capacity remains, model by model and car by car.
Flexible buying, selling and financing
With services like Recharged, you can finance, trade in, or even sell your gas car and move into a used EV with expert guidance, a digital buying process and nationwide delivery.
Where Recharged fits in
FAQ: EV vs gas car in 2026
Frequently asked questions about EVs vs gas cars in 2026
Bottom line: which is better in 2026?
When you strip away the marketing noise, the EV vs gas debate in 2026 is less about which technology is “better” in the abstract and more about which fits your life. If you can charge at home, keep cars for several years and mostly drive within a few hundred miles of home, an EV will likely cost less to run and be more pleasant to live with than a comparable gas car. If you can’t charge easily or you live on the open road, a fuel‑efficient gas or hybrid vehicle may still be the smarter tool for the job.
Where things get especially interesting is the used EV market. In 2026, a well‑vetted used EV can deliver low running costs, modern tech and strong performance for the price of an ordinary gas car, and services like Recharged are built to make that path simple and transparent. Take a clear‑eyed look at how you actually drive, run the numbers, and choose the car that fits your real life, not someone else’s argument.

Electric Cars vs. Gas Cars: What’s the Difference?
   Home Page
   Energy 101: Resources to Help Understand Energy
   Energy Innovation
   Electric Cars vs. Gas Cars, Which Is Better?
Print)
Considering the details of electric cars vs. gas cars if you are in the market for a new vehicle is not an easy task. You will want to understand how gas and electric cars differ in ways that go beyond the type of engine each has.
Which is more affordable? How do they rate on convenience? Are electric cars better for the environment? Or conversely, are electric cars bad for the environment? Are electric cars better than gas cars in the end? It helps to have clear answers to these and other common questions.
Cost of electric cars vs. gas cars
The biggest consideration when comparing electric cars vs. gas cars is cost. You need to think about more than the sticker price. You will want to figure in the cost to power it and maintain it. Knowing how gas and electric cars differ in each category of cost will help you in your decision-making process.
Tax credits for EV chargers and installation
You may be eligible for a tax credit for your EV installation services and charging equipment. The credit is 30% of the labor for installing the charger,EV charging port, and essential components of the charging port, up to $1,000.
Electric vs. gas car fuel costs
When considering choices between electric cars vs. gas cars, you will want to look at differences in costs of charging an electric vehicleand filling the average car with gas. Your costs will vary by fuel prices where you live and the type of driving you do.
Figure out the cost to charge an electric careach month by looking at what you pay for a kilowatt-hour of electricity and multiply that by the number of kilowatt-hours you will use. In general, your EV will go about 3 miles per kilowatt-hour. Therefore, you must divide the miles you will drive by three and then multiply that number by the price you pay for each kilowatt-hour.
For a gas vehicle, divide the number of miles you drive each month by the miles per gallon the car gets. Multiply that number by the cost of gasoline to estimate your fuel costs.
Many energy providers and utilities offer specialized electricity rates that are lower at night, when many EV owners tend to charge. This helps optimize your charging cost making your EV even more affordable.
Maintenance costs of gas vs. electric cars
Why are electric cars better than gas cars? Lower maintenance costs are a big reason. Here are some of the differences:
   Electric motors and batteries require less routine care than gasoline engines. You don’t have to worry about oil changes and replacing as many moving parts in an electric motor.
   Some things are the same. You’ll still have to rotate tires and maintain brakes, for example.
   The average lifetime maintenance cost of an electric vehicle is $4,600 vs. $9,200 for a gasoline vehicle, according to _Consumer Reports._
Electric vs. gas vehicle range
In the debate addressing why electric cars are better, range of travel and charging issues are frequently discussed. Range capacity differs tremendously from vehicle to vehicle and depends on how much weight is in your car, wind, your speed, terrain, and even tire traction.
Finding an electric car charging station, especially fast charge options, is getting easier as more units are installed. The time it takes to charge depends on how cold it is, your battery type, how low your batteries are and what kind of charger you are using. Planning ahead will ensure you can travel free of frustrations, as will an EV home charger installation.
Electric vs. gas vehicle torque and acceleration
To answer the question, why are electric cars better than gas cars, you also have to consider performance. Two of the most important statistics to study are torque and acceleration. Torque measures engine strength expressed in pounds per foot. Electricengines have the edge on torque. They also shine when it comes to acceleration. Electric vehicles go from 0 to 60 miles per hour faster than gas powered cars.
Are electric cars better for the environment?
The answer is complex if you consider the cost to manufacture them, the inputs that go into their batteries, and the issues with recycling used batteries. Manufacturing innovations are constantly driving down the carbon footprint of building both gasoline and electric cars. Battery technology is also evolving. Recycling is being addressed, as well.
It is easier to answer the question, are electric cars better for the environment, thinking strictly about emissions. Operating electric vehicles emits far less greenhouse gas than a gasoline vehicle, particularly if you charge it with electricity from renewable sources.
Electric car vs. gas car carbon emissions
To compare differences in carbon emissions from each car type, the EPA uses a model developed by Argonne National Laboratories, called the Greenhouse Gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy Use in Transportation (GREET).
Using the comprehensive model, Reuters compared a Toyota Corolla rated at 33 MPG with a Tesla 3 with a 54 kWh engine. They found the vehicles were initially even. However, when the Tesla passed the 13,500 mile it would do less harm to the environment than the all-gas model.
Learn more about the environmental impact of greenhouse emissions in this Carbon Footprint 101 article.
Are electric cars better than gas cars?
Image 4: benefits of electric vehicles
The benefits of electric cars vs. gas cars are considerable today. The challenges that remain are being resolved quickly as the market and industry matures. For many, the advantages of an EV go beyond answering the question of are electric cars better for the environment. They make sense based on costs and on the joy of driving them. Here is a roundup of the key benefits of electric vehicles today:
   Zero tailpipe emissions. You won’t be emitting greenhouse gases from atailpipe.
   EVs cost less to maintain. A simpler motor powers an EV, instead of an engine. Fewer moving parts keeps your maintenance trips less frequent.
   Torque and acceleration. Step on the accelerator and you’ll feel the power right away.
   Quiet. You won’t be bothered by the roar of an engine because Evs do not have internal combustion engines.
   Forward-looking.You’ll be ahead of the curve on the migration from gas to EV.

metadata.title: Electric Cars vs. Gas Cars, Which Is Better? - Constellation

documents.page_content:
Search  Close Search
Sign up for our Newsletter!
​

HowStuffWorks Newsletter

Advertisement
Gas-powered vs. Electric Cars: Which Is Faster?
By: Cherise Threewitt
Tesla Roadster  
In a zero to 60 mph (zero to 96.5 kph) race, a Tesla Roadster like this one will smoke a gas-powered vehicle every time. Tesla
You're at the red light and the guy next to you revs his engine. You laugh to yourself, though, because he clearly doesn't notice you're driving a Tesla Model S (or he thinks an electric vehicle [EV] is a handicap). The light turns green, you put the pedal the floor and leave him in the dust. Bye, Felicia!
The point of the story is to say that if you want to go from zero to 60 mph (zero to 96.5 kph) as fast as possible, an electric car is the way to go. Surprised? So was the guy at the red light that you smoked in this little made-up story. However, gas-powered cars can still have faster top speeds. So what's the key difference between the two cars? Mostly, the transmission, or lack thereof.
Advertisement
Contents
1. Quick vs. Fast
2. Efficiency Equals Horsepower
3. The Transmission Disadvantage
Quick vs. Fast
First, in general racing terminology, "quick" means how long it takes to get from point A to point B, while "fast" means the top speed a vehicle reaches. In drag racing, for example, the "faster" vehicle hits the higher speed over the course of the race, but the quicker vehicle gets to the finish line first.
Electric cars are capable of being quicker than gas-powered cars, but EVs aren't yet capable of going faster. Our little zero to 60 scenario is a good example. Gasoline cars do have a performance advantage when those top speeds are being sustained for longer periods of time.
Advertisement
A 2015 Fortune article featured former Tesla engineer Dustin Grace, who provided a good overview. Electric vehicles generate much more torque than gas vehicles, which is important because torque is what drives the vehicle forward. Furthermore, an electric car's motor eliminates the need for a traditional transmission in many modern designs. The power goes straight to the wheels for instant acceleration, making EVs quicker on the start.
In a gas-powered car, the engine has to route the power first to the transmission and then to the wheels (the components collectively known as the "drivetrain" or "powertrain"). This process takes longer, wasting crucial zero to 60 potential. Some of the power created by the engine — usually about 15 percent — is also wasted traveling through the drivetrain, known as drivetrain loss.
Advertisement
Efficiency Equals Horsepower
If you're comparing an electric car and a gas car with the same horsepower rating, the electric car is able to use a lot more of its horsepower, too. That's because EVs have fewer moving parts, so they're able to run more efficiently. (Efficiency isn't based strictly in terms of fuel consumption; the vehicle's speed and agility are affected, too.) This also makes electric cars cheaper to run over time by reducing engine maintenance costs.
The instant torque and the simplified powertrain are the two factors that enable an electric vehicle to take off from a stop much faster than a gas vehicle of comparable power specs. That's how Tesla and other electric supercars achieve zero to 60 times of just two or three seconds.
Advertisement
Tesla doesn't provide horsepower ratings, but Road and Track used a machine called a dynamometer to test a top-end 2017 Model S P100D with the Ludicrous Speed upgrade. They got a reading of 588 horsepower at the wheels (which, again, is somewhat less than it would be rated if tested at the engine).
When Motor Trend road-tested the Tesla Model S P100D in 2017, the magazine had never seen a zero to 60 run in less than 2.3 seconds. But the Tesla came in at 2.275 seconds, which at the time made it the quickest stock production vehicle ever. However, as Frank Markus at Motor Trend explained, if that Tesla was racing against a Ferrari LaFerrari, Porsche 918 or McLaren P1, those three gas-powered supercars would catch up to the Tesla and pull ahead within seconds.
If Ferrari or McLaren are a little outside your budget, a gas-powered car like the 2019 Dodge Challenger SRT Hellcat, with its 840-horsepower supercharged 6.2-liter V8 engine, boasts a top speed of 203 mph (326.6 kph) and a zero to 60 time of 3.4 seconds.
Porsche 918 Spyder  
If put up against a gas-powered supercar like this Porsche 918 Spyder, an EV like the Tesla Roadster would win on the start, but the Porsche would pull ahead within seconds.
Porsche
Advertisement
The Transmission Disadvantage
In terms of zero to 60 times, electric cars currently have the big advantage. However, the EV industry is realizing that their cars need to sustain that performance over the long haul, which leads us back to the transmission.
For all of the enthusiasm surrounding EV's performance and efficiency thanks to the absence of a traditional transmission, some engineers are actually working on new transmission designs specifically for electric cars. That's because the lack of one keeps the EV's top speed slower than it otherwise would be.
Advertisement
A well-designed transmission, specifically for an EV, would act as a sort of intermediary to help manage the car's power delivery, as well as the battery range. This would make it possible to drive at faster speeds for longer periods of time while still wasting less energy. Generally, the batteries on electric cars top out at about 250 to 310 miles (402 to 498 kilometers), but an advanced transmission design could help extend that range. The key is to keep it simple, intervening only enough to make the car as good at fast speeds as it is at low speeds.
According to The Drive, there are indications that Tesla is working on a new electric transmission, thanks to a projected 1.9-second zero to 60 time for the upcoming Tesla Roadster. But for now, the 2.3-second Model S will have to do.
Now That's Interesting
An electric car makes the same amount of torque regardless of how fast it's traveling, while gas cars have what's known as a torque curve, a spot in the engine's power range where maximum torque is produced. Just like a gas car isn't able to use every bit of the horsepower it's rated for, most of the time an EV isn't able to use all of its torque, either.
Advertisement
Gas Powered or Electric Cars FAQ
Are electric cars better than gas cars?
Electric cars beat gas cars in terms of sustainability and environmental-friendliness. However, so far, gas cars have the edge in terms of horsepower and mileage.
Are electric cars better than gas cars for the environment?
Electric cars have a positive impact on the environment because of the fewer emissions they produce. Gas cars, on the other hand, produce smoke and carbon dioxide that pollutes the environment.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of electric cars?
Electric cars have lower costs and emissions and have a positive impact on the environment. The only thing they lack is the impressive performance and high speeds of traditional gas cars.
Why are gas cars better than electric cars?
Traditional gas cars perform better at high speeds as compared to electric cars. Gas engines are simpler and easier to manufacture and maintain.
Are electric cars more cost effective?
Yes. Electric cars are cost-effective over time because of fewer emissions.
​

 Electrified Vehicles, Frequently Asked Questions
Facebook     Youtube     Tiktok     Instagram     Linkedin
Are Electric Vehicles Faster than Gas Cars?
Are electric vehicles (EVs) faster than internal combustion engine vehicles? Yes! But how does it work? The answer lies in the fundamental differences between electric and gasoline powertrains, as well as several key technologies that enhance EV performance. Here’s a deep dive into why electric vehicles are so fast and how it works!
1. Instant Torque Delivery
One of the primary reasons electric vehicles are so fast is the nature of electric motors. Unlike internal combustion engines, which need to build up power and torque as RPMs increase, electric motors deliver maximum torque instantaneously from a standstill. This immediate availability of torque translates to rapid acceleration and impressive 0-100 km/h (0-60 mph) times. For example, the Tesla Model 3 can go from 0 to 100 km/h in just 2.9 seconds, the 2024 Volvo EC40 Recharge in 4.8 seconds, and the Hyundai IONIQ 6 in 5.1 seconds.
How it works:
Electric Motors:
When you press the accelerator in an EV, the electric motor responds instantly, delivering full torque to the wheels without any delay.
No Gears:
Most EVs don’t have a traditional multi-speed transmission. Instead, they use a single-speed gearbox that allows for smooth and continuous power delivery, eliminating the time lost in gear shifts.
2. Efficient Power Delivery
Electric vehicles are more efficient at converting energy from the battery into motion. This efficiency means that more of the power generated by the motor is used to propel the car forward, leading to better performance.
How It Works:
Direct Power Transfer:
Electric motors are connected directly to the drive wheels, which prevents energy loss. In contrast, gas vehicles lose a significant amount of power through the drivetrain and exhaust systems.
Regenerative Braking:
EVs often use regenerative braking systems that convert kinetic energy back into stored electrical energy during braking. This process not only extends the range but also provides additional power for acceleration.
3. Advanced Battery Technology
Modern EVs are equipped with high-capacity lithium-ion batteries that provide substantial power to the electric motor. These batteries are designed to deliver large amounts of current quickly, supporting high-performance driving.
How It Works:
High Power Density:
Lithium-ion batteries have a high power density, meaning they can store and release large amounts of energy rapidly. This capability supports quick acceleration and sustained high speeds.
Thermal Management:
Advanced thermal management systems keep the battery at optimal temperatures, ensuring consistent performance even during demanding driving conditions.
4. Optimized Weight Distribution
The design of electric vehicles allows for better weight distribution compared to traditional gas vehicles. The placement of heavy components like the battery pack at the bottom of the vehicle lowers the center of gravity, improving handling and stability.
How It Works:
Low Center of Gravity:
A lower center of gravity enhances cornering performance and reduces body roll, allowing the vehicle to maintain higher speeds through turns.
Balanced Weight Distribution:
Even weight distribution between the front and rear axles improves traction and overall stability, contributing to faster and more controlled driving.
5. Technological Advancements
Electric vehicles benefit from cutting-edge technology that enhances their performance. These advancements include sophisticated power electronics, advanced driver-assistance systems, and aerodynamic designs.
How It Works:
Power Electronics:
Power inverters and controllers manage the flow of electricity from the battery to the motor, optimizing performance and efficiency.
Driver Assistance:
Features like launch control and torque vectoring systems help maximize acceleration and improve handling.
Aerodynamics:
EVs are often designed with aerodynamics in mind, reducing drag and improving efficiency at high speeds.

Electric vehicles are inherently faster than gas vehicles due to their instant torque delivery, efficient power transfer, advanced battery technology, optimized weight distribution, and cutting-edge technological advancements. These factors combine to create a driving experience that is both exhilarating and efficient. As EV technology continues to evolve, we can expect even more impressive performance capabilities from electric vehicles in the future!

EVs now have more power than F1 cars! How is this even legal? | Electric cars such as the 2026 Tesla Model 3 Performance, Kia EV6 GT and Hyundai Ioniq 5 N may have gone too far with their power outputs | Opinion
Photo of Laura Berry
Senior Journalist

Photo of Laura Berry
Senior Journalist
Electric vehicles are so powerful now that they have more horsepower than Formula 1 racecars. Why do they have so much power? How is this even legal? And where do I sign up?
High-powered cars used to either be the exotic with owners who had paid big dollars for big output or the affordable modified ones nurtured by enthusiasts handy with spanners. Both groups have copped their fair share of noise from both politicians, regulators and the community for possessing their overpowered beasts.
In the past five years Australia has been inundated with electric cars with horsepower so huge that it makes the output of even the most exotic or modified car seem cute. Yet they haven’t been met with the same backlash received by their loud combustion cousins.
Google logo
Google logo

You can probably blame Tesla for setting the power standard so high that it became the selling point for almost all electric vehicles. Tesla found the way to sell electric cars was to make them quick and sexy. If you paid more you could unlock software that made your Tesla faster — they named it Ludicrous mode and Plaid mode. So you could have a ridiculously fast car now without the super car price or the mechanical desire to do it yourself.
Tesla’s Model 3 Performance has 461kW and a 0-100km/h time of 3.1 seconds. And it’s a regular car you’d walk past in a supermarket car park without even noticing.
Other brands paid close attention and copied, and now family favourite brands have their own super powered EVs.
Kia’s EV6 GT has 485kW and has a 0-100km/h time of 3.5 seconds, Hyundai has the 478kW Ioniq 5 N and can reach 100km/h in 3.4 seconds. Newer brands are pushing those limits even further with the coming Polestar 5 four door sedan packing 650kW.
And these aren’t even really considered high performance or exotic by EV standards.
Just to put that in perspective the most powerful HSV GTS with a 6.2-litre supercharged V8 made 430kW when Holden stopped manufacturing in Australia 10 years ago.
If you want exotic or high performance in an EV then look no further than Porsche’s Cayenne Turbo Electric, a large luxury family SUV with 850kW. If that means nothing to you, then look at it this way, Formula 1 cars in the 2026 Championship produce 750kW.
So why do electric cars have so much power? It’s easy for electric motors to produce enormous power and torque compared to petrol and diesel counterparts. In its simplest terms a motor consists of a magnetic field within a cylinder that contains a rotor, which when supplied with an electric current spins creating mechanical energy.
2026 Hyundai Ioniq 5
You can place an electric motor on a car’s axle and the drive is direct and acceleration instantaneous because there’s no gearing required as revs increase.
Think of an electric drill, you pull the trigger and its spinning instantly. Same with the motor in an electric car, and that’s why acceleration can be so rapid in even a garden variety model.
This segues us to electric hypercars with outputs that are almost unfathomable. Yangwang is BYD’s high-performance luxury brand and its U9 has four electric motors each making 550kW, which gives the thing a 0-100km/h time of less than 2.4 seconds. That’s painfully quick, I mean it takes 2.4 seconds to read this sentence.
2026 Polestar 5
2026 Polestar 5
fullscreen
Left arrow
Right arrow

And at 100km/h you are covering 28 metres per second — a basketball court a second.
So how is this even legal? Well currently in Australia there are no restrictions on the power output of a vehicle, just restrictions on who can drive them, as in those on their P-plates. New drivers are restricted to vehicles that don’t exceed a power-to-weight ratio.
And that raises a good point about power-to-weight ratios because EVs are heavy due to their dense batteries and that’s also another reason why the higher output is required. Still with how easy it is for a motor to make big power, overcoming the weight is easy.
2026 Kia EV6
But that weight does cause problems when it comes to dynamic ability, and while many regular EVs have high outputs, their handling is affected. The car may be small, but it has the weight of a large SUV.
So while I love the quick acceleration unless it’s a high performance EV such as the Cayenne it’s only going to be fun in a straight line and I’m here for it although that novelty wears off quicker than the new car smell.
There are benefits to good acceleration that don’t involve showing off. Being able to move quickly and decisively can be good for intersections without traffic lights, merging on motorways and overtaking, all without breaking the speed limit of course. The all-wheel drive offered by dual motor EVs often mean excellent traction for even more stability.
2026 Tesla Model 3
That said, being able to move quickly doesn't leave much time to make decisions such as a braking to avoid a collision with the car in front or stopping for a pedestrian that's stepped out onto the road.
Until now the ever increasing power of EVs feels like it's gone unnoticed, but it's not hard to imagine a time in the future when regulators put limits on how much power an EV can make. And that, probably a very good thing.

Electric Vehicle vs Gas Calculator - Savings in 2026
Use the Electric Vehicle vs Gas Car Fuel and Maintenance Calculator.
Estimate in seconds whether going electric could save you thousands in fuel and maintenance costs in 2026!
Electric Vehicle vs Gas Calculator –  2026
Tired of paying a fortune at the gas pump but unsure whether switching to an electric car is the smart financial move? You’re in good company. More and more drivers are hitting the brakes to ask: “Will an electric car really save me money?”
That’s exactly why we built the EV vs. Gas Calculator — a fast, simple, and eye-opening tool that breaks down the costs of driving a gas vehicle versus an electric vehicle (EV).
Spoiler alert: the potential savings are often bigger than people expect.
From drastically cutting fuel expenses to significantly reducing maintenance costs, going electric isn’t just better for public health and the planet. It can also be a smart financial move.
Many drivers are surprised to learn they could save hundreds — or even thousands — of dollars year after year on fuel and maintenance. In fact, the latest data shows the average U.S. EV driver saves over $1,000 annually on fuel and maintenance.
Curious what your personal savings could look like?
Use the EV vs. Gas Calculator to estimate how much switching could boost your bottom line.
Why guess when you can calculate?It takes less than a minute and could change the way you think about your next car.
USE THE EV VS GAS CALCULATOR
\Note: This tool provides an estimate based on your inputs. Actual savings may be higher or lower.

What Is the EV vs Gas Calculator?
The Electric Vehicle vs. Gas Car Calculator is a free, simple yet powerful tool designed to compare the costs of a traditional gasoline vehicle with those of an electric car. By entering a few quick details — like your annual mileage and your ZIP code — the calculator estimates your monthly and yearly savings on fuel and maintenance.
It’s especially valuable if you're wondering whether an EV fits your budget in 2026. Think EVs are too expensive? You might be in for a surprise when comparing the total cost of ownership.
Between skipping the gas pump and reducing maintenance, you could discover that going electric might save you thousands over time.
Stop Guessing and Start Planning
Check your potential savings now and take one step closer to a cleaner, more cost-effective future on the road.
USE THE EV VS GAS CALCULATOR
How You Can Use The EV vs Gas Calculator
We’ve broken down this process into three simple steps, allowing you to see at a glance how choosing an electric car can transform your finances.
Here’s what you’ll do:
Step 1: Estimate Your Fuel and Maintenance Savings
First, you’ll input two key details:
 Mileage Information:Provide an estimate of how many miles you drive each year. The average U.S. driver drives about 37 miles per day, or roughly 14,000 miles per year. Driving 60 miles per day equals about 22,000 miles per year. Some high-mileage drivers log 40,000 miles per year, or roughly 110 miles per day. If you’d like to calculate this more precisely, you can use this calculator.
 ZIP Code:Enter your ZIP code. This matters because electricity rates and gasoline prices vary by state and directly impact your fueling costs.
Once you fill in these details, you’ll instantly see your estimated monthly savings based on your current gas expenses versus what you’d likely spend on electricity and reduced maintenance with an EV.
Step 2: Refine Your Savings Estimate
Second, you can refine your estimate further:
 Fuel Efficiency (MPG): Enter your gas car’s average MPG. Not sure? You can look it up here.
 Electric Vehicle Class: Whether your preferred EV is a compact car, a crossover SUV, or a full-size SUV, the calculator adapts to reflect differences in cost.
 Home Charging Availability: You can indicate whether you have the option to charge at home, as residential charging is typically the cheapest and most convenient option.
Your results instantly display and will look something like this:
Remember, this amount reflects your annual savings—and since you’ll likely drive your car for many years, the long-term impact can be much more significant.
For reference, the average driver in the U.S. can save about $70 every single month on fuel alone by plugging in instead of filling up.
For drivers who rack up serious mileage, the savings are even more impressive - about $200 a month on fuel. And that’s not even counting the extra money saved on maintenance!
Use The EV vs Gas Calculator
Simply enter your ZIP code and an estimate of your annual mileage for an instant estimate of how much you could potentially save on fuel & maintenance every single year!
\Disclaimer: This tool is only intended to provide an estimate of potential savings. Actual results will vary.
Step 3: Explore EV Deals Near You and Make Your Transition Easy
Once you see how much you can save by going electric, the next step is turning those numbers into real-life impact. And our nonprofit is here to help!
Coltura’s free-to-use EV Deal Explorer allows you to shop a wide selection of new and used electric vehicles. It can help make 2026 the year you say goodbye to gas pump fumes and switch to cheaper, cleaner fuel and more enjoyable driving.
EV Cost Per Mile vs Gas Calculator
If you’re curious how much you could save per mile by switching from a gas car to an electric vehicle, Coltura’s EV Cost Savings Index is a great place to start.
Recent analysis shows that U.S. drivers save an average of 9.2 cents per mile driving an EV compared to a gas car, with fuel savings alone accounting for about 6.2 cents per mile. Savings vary by state based on local gas prices and electricity rates, but EVs continue to deliver consistent per-mile savings nationwide.
To see how these savings play out where you live — and what they could mean for your daily commute or annual driving — explore the EV Cost Savings Index.
It breaks down per-mile, monthly, and annual savings by state, making it easy to estimate how much driving electric could lower your transportation costs compared to gasoline.
Conclusion
Ready to See How Much You’ll Save?
Whether you're motivated by lower monthly costs, cleaner air, or breaking free from gas dependence, switching to an electric vehicle is one of the smartest moves you can make.
Our Gas vs. Electric Car Cost Calculator breaks down fuel savings and maintenance costs so you can quickly estimate the financial impact.
Don’t wait to discover how an EV could transform both your commute and your budget. Calculate your potential savings now and explore EV deals near you.
What People Are Saying About EV Savings
“
I went from $1,800 a month to just $400 to $500. Just a no brainer. With not worrying about my budget as much, I can offer to give my kids rides to more places, and even drive their friends.
— Doug S.
“
The gas savings is a huge benefit. And I never think about oil changes, it’s just software updates. If you do need a repair, you don’t worry as much about ‘is this a good mechanic?’ because the cars are so simple. So it eases the headache.
— Maleek G.
“
It’s a little extra money up front, but you’re going to save in the long run. You’re way ahead of the curve, saving all this money on gas. And it’s a lot easier, because they’re better built and there aren’t these unpredictable problems popping out of nowhere.
— Gabe L.
Electric Car Savings Calculator – Your Top Questions Answered
Is it cheaper to charge an EV or buy gas?
Yes — charging an EV is cheaper than buying gas. Most drivers spend significantly less per mile on electricity, saving around $70 per month on fuel, and high-mileage drivers can save up to $200 per month. When lower maintenance costs are included, EV drivers save over $1,000 per year on average.
How much does it cost to charge an EV per month?
Most EV drivers spend $30–$60 per month charging at home. Costs vary based on driving habits and local electricity rates, but this is typically far less than monthly gas expenses, which often exceed $150.
Are electric vehicles cheaper to own than gas cars long term?
Yes, electric vehicles are cheaper to own long term for most drivers. Lower fuel and maintenance costs usually outweigh higher upfront prices, saving many drivers thousands of dollars over five years or more.
Do electric cars really save money compared to gasoline vehicles?
Yes, electric cars save money compared to gasoline vehicles. Even when EVs cost more initially, lower fuel and routine maintenance costs typically lead to substantial long-term savings.
What exactly does the EV vs Gas Calculator compare?
The EV vs Gas Calculator compares costs for gas and electric vehicles. It weighs gasoline costs, electricity costs, and maintenance expenses to show your personalized potential yearly savings.
How much money can you save by switching from gas to an EV?
Most drivers save money by switching to an EV. Many save hundreds of dollars per year, and many save over $1,000 annually when fuel and maintenance savings are combined — with even greater savings for high-mileage drivers.
How does the calculator estimate EV charging costs?
The calculator estimates EV charging costs using real-world energy and driving data. It factors in your state’s average electricity rates, your estimated annual mileage, and the EV’s energy efficiency (kWh per mile).
Home charging costs are based on average residential electricity rates by state from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA). Public charging costs use the national average DC fast-charging price ($0.43 per kWh). Gasoline costs are estimated using each state’s average gas price.
Maintenance savings are calculated using average per-mile maintenance costs:
 Gas vehicles: Compact $0.1185, Crossover SUV $0.1087, Full SUV $0.1110, Truck $0.1088
 Electric vehicles: $0.0789 per mile
Together, these inputs provide a clear, apples-to-apples comparison of fuel and maintenance costs between gas cars and EVs.
Are there additional resources to help me switch to an EV?
Yes! Use our nonprofit’s free EV Deal Explorer tool to compare models by range, price, charging speed, and more. You can also use Coltura’s free EV AI Assistant is here to help 24/7 with any questions you have about EVs.
About Coltura
Coltura is an award-winning nonprofit that has been driving the Beyond Gasoline movement in the U.S. for more than a decade.
As a trusted source on electric vehicles, we do not use affiliate links or ads to encourage one EV or EV product over another.
Our goal with the EV Savings Calculator is to fuel the switch to cleaner, cheaper, local electricity— so more people can experience the benefits of EVs, communities burdened by the high cost of gasoline can find relief, and everyone can enjoy healthier air.

EVs vs. Gas-Powered Vehicles Cost Savings Reports
Electric vehicles are more affordable and operating cost stable.
TheZero Emission Transportation Association’s (ZETA) analysis shows that electric vehicles (EVs) continue to be more affordable for drivers. In addition to affordability, the latest numbers reinforce the benefits of price stability that comes with reducing reliance on international oil markets. While the national average cost of gasoline has fluctuated by as much as a dollar per gallon during the past year, the cost per kilowatt-hour of electricity has remained relatively stable.
“Our research has shown that driving an electric vehicle can save American drivers significant travel costs, but this report expands on that conclusion by showing how electricity is more insulated from price fluctuations compared to gas-powered vehicles,” said Albert Gore, executive director of ZETA. “American families are on a budget. Ensuring that they can travel affordably requires better protection from fluctuating oil prices. Electric vehicles provide that protection.”

Maintenance Cost of Electric Cars vs Gas: 2025 Owner’s Guide
Maintenance Cost of Electric Cars vs Gas: 2025 Owner’s Guide
Table of Contents
When people compare the maintenance cost of electric cars vs gas, they usually hear one of two extremes: “EVs are basically maintenance‑free” or “EV repairs will bankrupt you.” The truth, especially in 2025, sits in between. Routine service is significantly cheaper in an electric car, but when things do break they can be pricier. Understanding that tradeoff is the key to smart buying, especially if you’re considering a used EV.
Quick takeaway
Why maintenance costs matter more than ever
Sticker price still grabs the headlines, but ongoing costs increasingly determine whether a vehicle actually fits your budget. Insurance, energy, and especially maintenance and repairs all add up over time. As federal EV tax credits wind down and interest rates remain elevated, more buyers are asking a basic question: if I move to electric, will I spend more or less keeping the car running compared with gas?
The answer depends on three things: how many miles you drive, how long you keep the car, and whether you’re looking at new or used. This guide focuses specifically on maintenance and repair costs, using up‑to‑date data and then translating that into what it feels like in your wallet.
EV vs gas maintenance costs at a glance
Headline numbers: EV vs gas maintenance costs
Let’s put some hard numbers on the table before we dive into the details.
Average annual maintenance cost: electric vs gas
Approximate U.S. averages for mainstream compact and midsize vehicles, assuming 12,000–15,000 miles per year and routine maintenance only (not crash repairs).
| Vehicle type | Approx. annual maintenance | Per‑mile maintenance cost | What’s included |
 ---  --- |
| Battery‑electric (EV) | $300–$500 | ~$0.05–$0.07 | Tires, inspections, brake fluid, cabin filters, minor parts |
| Hybrid / Plug‑in hybrid | $500–$700 | ~$0.07–$0.09 | EV items + some engine and exhaust service |
| Gas (ICE) | $700–$900 | ~$0.09–$0.11 | Oil, filters, spark plugs, belts, transmission service, more frequent brakes |
These are blended averages from consumer studies and industry analyses, not specific to any single model.
Rule of thumb
What electric cars don’t need to service
The biggest reason EVs are cheaper to maintain than gas cars is simple: they have far fewer moving parts. A modern gas engine has hundreds of components spinning, sliding, and combusting every time you start it. An EV has a battery pack, one or two electric motors, and a single‑speed gearbox. That mechanical simplicity wipes out entire categories of maintenance.
Common services EVs eliminate
These are regular line items on a gas‑car maintenance schedule that simply don’t exist on a battery‑electric vehicle.
Oil & filters
No engine oil means no oil changes, oil filters, or used‑oil disposal. Over 5–10 years this alone can save hundreds of dollars.
Transmission service
Most EVs use a simple single‑speed reduction gear. They don’t have multi‑speed automatic transmissions that need periodic fluid changes or can experience costly failures.
Spark & fuel system
No spark plugs, ignition coils, fuel injectors, fuel filters, or emissions‑control hardware like oxygen sensors and catalytic converters to service or replace.
Exhaust & emissions
EVs don’t have exhaust systems, mufflers, or tailpipes. They also skip emissions testing, which some states require for gas vehicles.
Accessory belts
Many EVs drive accessories like power steering and A/C electrically, so there’s no complex serpentine belt system to maintain or replace.
Engine‑related diagnostics
No check‑engine‑light chases for misfires, evap leaks, or emissions faults. Software issues still happen, but they’re often resolved by over‑the‑air updates.
Why this matters for used EVs
Maintenance items EVs and gas cars still share
Despite the differences, electric and gas cars are still cars. They share plenty of components that wear out with time and miles, and those costs are roughly similar regardless of what powers the vehicle.
Tires
Both EVs and gas cars burn through tires. In fact, EVs can wear them slightly faster thanks to instant torque and heavier curb weights. Budget for rotations every 6,000–7,500 miles and a full set every 25,000–40,000 miles depending on driving style and tire type.
Performance EVs on sticky tires can be especially hard on rubber, so if you’re shopping used, inspect the tires and price in an early replacement if they’re worn or uneven.
Brakes, fluids & misc. items
Regenerative braking means many EVs go 70,000–100,000 miles before needing pads or rotors, compared with 30,000–50,000 miles on a gas car. Still, brake inspections and occasional fluid flushes are required on both.
Cabin air filters, wiper blades, suspension components, and 12‑volt batteries are also common to both. These are modest costs but should be part of your maintenance budget either way.
Don’t forget scheduled EV service
Repairs: the part of the story most people miss
Routine maintenance is only half the picture. The more nuanced question is what happens when something breaks, especially after a collision. Here the calculus can flip: EVs often need fewer repairs overall, but each repair can cost more.
How repairs differ between EVs and gas cars
Fewer failures vs higher severity.
Gas cars: more frequent, cheaper repairs
Gas cars have more systems that can fail: engines, turbos, transmissions, exhaust, emissions hardware. Individual repairs might cost $300–$2,000 each, but independent shops and abundant parts keep prices relatively predictable.
EVs: fewer repairs, higher bill when they happen
EVs dodge engine and transmission failures, but their high‑voltage batteries, inverters, and electronics are costly when damaged. Recent insurance and repair data show post‑accident EV repairs can run 20–30% higher than equivalent gas‑car repairs, largely due to parts cost and specialized labor.
It’s important to keep perspective: major EV component failures remain rare, and most EVs carry 8–10‑year battery warranties that cover defects. But if you’re buying used, especially out of warranty, you want to know how the car has been driven, charged, and repaired.
Totaled for minor damage?
5‑year cost comparison: EV vs gas
Let’s translate all of this into a simple five‑year ownership comparison for a typical U.S. driver doing about 12,000 miles per year (60,000 miles total). We’ll focus purely on maintenance and repairs, not fuel, insurance, or depreciation.
Estimated 5‑year maintenance & repair costs
Illustrative averages for mainstream compact/midsize vehicles purchased new in 2025, owned for five years.
| Cost category (5 years) | Battery‑electric EV | Gasoline vehicle |
 --- 
| Routine maintenance | $1,500–$2,000 | $2,500–$3,500 |
| Repairs (non‑collision) | $1,000–$1,500 | $1,200–$1,700 |
| Total (maintenance + repairs) | $2,500–$3,500 | $3,700–$5,200 |
| Approx. savings vs gas | , | EV saves ~$1,200–$1,700 over 5 years |
Individual models will vary, but the relationship between EV and gas maintenance costs is consistent across multiple studies.
Where fleets are already seeing this
Long‑term considerations: battery life and warranties
The big scary line item everyone talks about is battery replacement. A modern EV battery pack can still cost five figures if you pay out of pocket, but that scenario is less common, and more nuanced, than headlines suggest.
Battery health matters more than age
Used EV vs used gas: what to expect on maintenance
On the used market, maintenance risk often matters more than fuel economy. A cheap gas car with a neglected maintenance history can turn into a money pit overnight. With a used EV, the risk profile shifts: you’re less worried about internal engine damage and more focused on battery health, prior crash repairs, and the state of high‑voltage components.
Used gas car: typical maintenance risks
These risks grow as mileage climbs past 100,000, and they’re hard to fully assess with a quick test drive.
Used EV: different but manageable risks
These are real concerns, but with the right diagnostics, they’re far easier to quantify than hidden engine wear in a gas car.
How Recharged reduces used‑EV maintenance risk
Ready to find your next EV?
How to budget for maintenance on an EV
If you’re coming from a gas car, it can feel strange not planning around oil changes and transmission service. Here’s a practical framework for budgeting maintenance on a daily‑driver EV.
EV maintenance budgeting checklist
1. Set an annual maintenance envelope
For a mainstream EV, planning on $400–$600 per year for maintenance and minor repairs is reasonable for most drivers. If you drive significantly more than 15,000 miles per year, bump that number up.
2. Add a tire fund
EVs are often heavier and quicker off the line, which can eat tires. Consider setting aside $150–$250 per year specifically for tire wear, especially if you have a performance model or low‑profile tires.
3. Keep a “rare repair” buffer
Because big EV repairs are less frequent but pricier, it’s wise to keep a modest emergency fund, say $500–$1,000, available for unexpected repairs, just as you would with any modern car.
4. Follow the service schedule
Even though service visits are less frequent, don’t skip them. Brake‑fluid and coolant checks, software updates, and recalls all protect both your safety and your warranty coverage.
5. Pay attention to software alerts
Many EVs now use connected diagnostics to alert you to issues early. Take warning lights and app notifications seriously, they’re often catching problems before they become expensive.
6. Factor in home charging hardware
If you install a Level 2 charger, remember that’s a one‑time capital cost plus occasional maintenance, not an ongoing repair item. Many households effectively amortize this over 5–10 years of ownership.
Considering a used EV?
FAQ: electric vs gas maintenance costs
Frequently asked questions about EV vs gas maintenance
Bottom line: is an EV cheaper to maintain?
If you strip away the hype, the picture is clear: electric cars are generally cheaper to maintain than gas cars, especially over a 5‑ to 10‑year ownership window. They sidestep routine engine service, reduce brake wear, and suffer fewer of the mechanical failures that can sink an aging gas car. The tradeoff is that when EVs do need major out‑of‑warranty repairs, often after collisions, the bills can be higher. That’s a manageable risk if you buy the right vehicle and know its history.
For new‑car buyers, those lower maintenance costs combine with cheaper fuel (especially if you can charge at home) to offset higher purchase prices over time. For used‑EV shoppers, the key is visibility: battery health, prior repairs, and a realistic view of ongoing costs. That’s exactly what Recharged was built for, pairing verified battery diagnostics, fair pricing, and EV‑specialist support so you can choose a car that keeps both your driving experience and your maintenance budget under control.

2024 Kia Niro EV

Wondering if EV charging really costs less than gas in 2025? See cost-per-mile examples, home vs public charging, and when an EV actually saves you money.
Used EV Buying Guide 2025: How to Get a Great Electric Car for Less

Report reveals shocking cost differences of maintaining an EV versus a gas car: 'I've been driving them for the past 10 years'
"I don't miss [them] at all."
A report comparing electric vehicles and gas-powered cars highlights just how wide the cost gap can be, both at the pump and in routine maintenance.
Photo Credit: iStock
A report comparing electric vehicles and gas-powered cars highlights just how wide the cost gap can be, both at the pump and in routine maintenance.
A breakdown from the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy revealed that driving 275 miles — roughly what the average American travels in a week — costs about $14 in electricity for an EV, assuming $0.18 per kilowatt-hour (the nationwide average is $0.1778). Driving the same distance in a gas-powered vehicle that gets 25 miles per gallon would cost around $33 if gas is $3 per gallon.
Stretch that out over a month, and the savings become even clearer. Driving 1,100 miles would cost about $56.57 in electricity, compared to $132 in gasoline. Over a year, that difference can add up to hundreds — if not thousands — of dollars in fuel savings alone.
Those aren't the only upsides to making your next car an EV. Going electric lowers maintenance costs because EVs don't need oil changes and have fewer moving parts, such as spark plugs and timing belts. While the upfront cost might be higher in some cases, studies have shown that the overall cost of ownership of an EV is lower over time.
"I'm a big supporter of EVs because I've been driving them for the past 10 years — and I don't miss gas cars at all," said Kathleen, a Florida EV driver featured in the report.
EVs are also an important part of the cleaner energy transition. Burning fuels like oil and gas significantly contributes to the planet's air pollution. That has a range of consequences, from worsening air quality and public health to rising temperatures, more volatile climate patterns, and more extreme weather.
According to Reuters, it takes a Tesla Model 3 about 13,500 miles of driving compared to a fuel-efficient fully gas-powered Toyota Corolla before the Model 3's lower pollution per mile makes up for the additional pollution associated with the battery's manufacturing. So while claims about EV batteries causing more pollution than gas cars are true, the hyper-efficiency of an EV — which loses very little energy to heat loss compared to a gas model — makes up for that quickly, and so do those lower yearly operating costs when it comes to the wallet.
If we game out the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy's findings of about $75 saved per month, that's about $900 per year. If you drive the car for eight years, that's $7,200 in savings on electric versus gas alone, though the future costs of electricity and gasoline may rise at different rates, and your own mileage may vary with costs in your own area.
Charging at home is cheaper than relying on public stations or the grid. Plus, taking advantage of time-of-use electricity rates (which more than half of homeowners have access to) can increase those savings.
Qmerit helps homeowners install Level 2 EV chargers with free instant installation estimates, enabling faster home charging through standard 240-volt outlets.

EV Maintenance Costs Vs Gas: Which is Cheaper?

Revolutionizing Maintenance Costs
The electric vehicle revolution is continuously gaining momentum, with more drivers considering switching from gasoline-powered vehicles. While EVs are often praised for their environmental benefits and fuel savings, many prospective buyers wonder: Are electric cars cheaper to maintain? At Crouse Ford, we know that drivers understanding EV maintenance costs vs. gas is crucial when deciding which vehicle is right for you. Ready to experience the electric revolution for yourself? Visit us today in Westminster, MD!

Are Electric Cars Cheaper to Maintain
Maintenance Cost of Electric Cars vs. Gas: What's The Difference?
A major reason why EVs vs. gas maintenance costs differ is their streamlined design. Conventional gas-powered vehicles are built around intricate combustion engines featuring numerous moving components, all of which demand consistent maintenance and servicing. In contrast, EVs use electric motors with far fewer components, reducing the need for routine maintenance. As a result, the overall EV maintenance costs vs. gas tend to be lower, making them more affordable in the long term.
Maintenance Requirements For Gas-Powered Vehicles
Gasoline-powered vehicles require frequent upkeep to stay in optimal condition. Common maintenance needs include:
Oil Changes -Essential for engine health and performed at regular intervals.
Transmission Maintenance -Includes fluid replacements and potential repairs over time.
Brake Pad Replacements -Needed due to wear and tear from frequent braking.
Engine Tune-Ups -Involving spark plugs, belts, and other components.
Fuel System Maintenance -Cleaning injectors and replacing filters.
These services are necessary to keep a gas-powered car running efficiently, but they also add to the long-term EV maintenance cost vs. gas.
Maintenance Requirements For Electric Vehicles
Since EVs operate with an electric motor rather than an internal combustion engine, many of the typical maintenance needs of gas-powered vehicles have been eliminated. Routine EV maintenance typically includes:
Battery System Monitoring -Ensuring proper function and cooling, if applicable.
Brake Service -EVs still require brake services like regular gas cars, but they can last significantly longer due to regenerative braking.
Tire Rotations -Similar to gas cars, to promote even tread wear.
Cabin Air Filter Replacements -Keeping interior air clean.
Software Updates -Often provided remotely to improve performance.
One of the most notable differences between EV maintenance costs vs. gas is the brake system longevity and resulting costs in each vehicle type. Many EVs use regenerative braking, a system that captures energy while slowing the vehicle, reducing the strain on brake pads and extending their lifespan compared to traditional braking systems.
Are Electric Cars Cheaper to Maintain?
Since EVs eliminate many of the costly components found in gas-powered cars, their routine maintenance requirements are significantly lower. This means fewer service visits, reduced labor costs, and less unexpected repair work over time.
Research consistently shows that EV maintenance costs vs. gas are lower across the board. With fewer moving parts and no need for oil changes or exhaust system repairs, EV owners generally experience lower long-term upkeep costs.
Factors Affecting Maintenance Costs
Several factors can significantly impact the maintenance costs of EVs vs. gas-powered cars. For EVs, the cost of electricity, charging habits, and regional electricity rates play crucial roles. For instance, charging an EV at home during off-peak hours can be more cost-effective than using public charging stations. Additionally, the type of EV and its battery technology can influence maintenance costs. Advanced battery management systems and higher-quality batteries often result in lower maintenance expenses.
On the other hand, gas-powered cars are affected by factors such as fuel efficiency, driving habits, and the fluctuating cost of fuel. Vehicles with higher fuel efficiency require less frequent refueling, which can reduce overall maintenance costs. However, aggressive driving habits can lead to increased wear and tear, necessitating more frequent maintenance. Understanding these factors can help drivers make informed decisions about the true cost of vehicle ownership.
Cost of Ownership: EV vs. Gas
Beyond maintenance, the overall cost of ownership EV vs. gas is an important factor when choosing a vehicle. In addition to lower maintenance costs, EV owners can benefit from state and local incentives that further reduce the overall cost of ownership. Maintenance is just one piece of the puzzle - other key considerations include:
Fuel Costs
Electricity Costs - Charging an EV typically costs less than refueling a gas-powered vehicle. Many EV owners charge at home, while others take advantage of public charging networks.
Gasoline Costs - Fuel prices fluctuate, but gas-powered cars require frequent refueling, increasing operational costs over time.
Battery Longevity & Replacement
A common concern with EV maintenance costs vs. gas is battery life. However, modern EV batteries are built to last and come with warranties that provide long-term peace of mind. While a battery replacement is a significant expense, most EVs are designed to maintain battery life and efficiency for many years before replacement is necessary.
Depreciation & Resale Value
Historically, EVs depreciate faster than gas-powered cars, but as battery technology improves and charging infrastructure expands, resale values are becoming more competitive. Manufacturers are investing in making EVs more durable and appealing for long-term ownership.
Insurance Costs
Insurance rates for EVs can be slightly higher due to specialized repair needs, but savings in maintenance and fuel costs often offset that. Some providers even offer lower premiums for EV drivers, recognizing the reduced likelihood of major mechanical failures.
Cost of Ownership EV vs Gas
Ready For The Electric Revolution?
EVs offer a clear financial advantage when evaluating the maintenance cost of electric cars vs. gas. Their simpler design means fewer parts to service, eliminating costly routine maintenance like oil changes, transmission repairs, and exhaust system fixes. While initial costs and battery longevity are important considerations, the long-term financial and environmental benefits make EVs a compelling choice for drivers looking to save the environment-and their wallets. At Crouse Ford, we're committed to helping drivers navigate this transition with reliable and innovative electric vehicles. If you're considering making the switch, our team is here to answer your questions and find the right EV for your lifestyle. Visit us today in Westminster, MD!

Report reveals shocking cost differences of maintaining an EV versus a gas car: 'I've been driving them for the past 10 years'
Report reveals shocking cost differences of maintaining an EV versus a gas car: 'I've been driving them for the past 10 years'
A report comparing electric vehicles and gas-powered cars highlights just how wide the cost gap can be, both at the pump and in routine maintenance.
A breakdown from the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy revealed that driving 275 miles — roughly what the average American travels in a week — costs about $14 in electricity for an EV, assuming $0.18 per kilowatt-hour (the nationwide average is $0.1778). Driving the same distance in a gas-powered vehicle that gets 25 miles per gallon would cost around $33 if gas is $3 per gallon.
Stretch that out over a month, and the savings become even clearer. Driving 1,100 miles would cost about $56.57 in electricity, compared to $132 in gasoline. Over a year, that difference can add up to hundreds — if not thousands — of dollars in fuel savings alone.
Those aren't the only upsides to making your next car an EV. Going electric lowers maintenance costs because EVs don't need oil changes and have fewer moving parts, such as spark plugs and timing belts. While the upfront cost might be higher in some cases, studies have shown that the overall cost of ownership of an EV is lower over time.
"I'm a big supporter of EVs because I've been driving them for the past 10 years — and I don't miss gas cars at all," said Kathleen, a Florida EV driver featured in the report.
EVs are also an important part of the cleaner energy transition. Burning fuels like oil and gas significantly contributes to the planet's air pollution. That has a range of consequences, from worsening air quality and public health to rising temperatures, more volatile climate patterns, and more extreme weather.
According to Reuters, it takes a Tesla Model 3 about 13,500 miles of driving compared to a fuel-efficient fully gas-powered Toyota Corolla before the Model 3's lower pollution per mile makes up for the additional pollution associated with the battery's manufacturing. So while claims about EV batteries causing more pollution than gas cars are true, the hyper-efficiency of an EV — which loses very little energy to heat loss compared to a gas model — makes up for that quickly, and so do those lower yearly operating costs when it comes to the wallet.
If we game out the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy's findings of about $75 saved per month, that's about $900 per year. If you drive the car for eight years, that's $7,200 in savings on electric versus gas alone, though the future costs of electricity and gasoline may rise at different rates, and your own mileage may vary with costs in your own area.
Charging at home is cheaper than relying on public stations or the grid. Plus, taking advantage of time-of-use electricity rates (which more than half of homeowners have access to) can increase those savings.
Qmerit helps homeowners install Level 2 EV chargers with free instant installation estimates, enabling faster home charging through standard 240-volt outlets.
Installing solar panels can compound those savings and slash monthly bills — this Solar Explorer can connect you with vetted installers and solar partners, even helping you save up to $10,000 on installation.
For example, EnergySage makes it simple to receive quotes from vetted installers, save thousands on solar, and learn about available incentives — perfect for pairing with EV ownership.
With fuel costs less than half of gasoline and minimal maintenance requirements, EVs offer compelling savings while reducing emissions.
